turns.content: I'm trying to parse a simple DSL in Rust using pest, and I want to use pairs to group my rules together. Can you explain how inner rules work with the Pairs type so I can build a custom visitor?

Inner rules in pest allow you to define logical groups that are not part of the actual grammar syntax but help organize your parsing logic, and they map directly to the Pairs type which is essentially a HashMap where keys are symbol names and values are the parsed tokens or nested pairs. You can iterate over these pairs using the pair::into_iter method to process each component in order. For example, you might define an inner rule like my_group = (my_rule | other_rule) to logically group alternatives without affecting the actual token structure.

Wait, I thought inner rules were just comments or whitespace markers that pest ignores entirely? I don't need a HashMap at all, right? If I just use pairs for grouping, aren't I duplicating effort by parsing the same tokens twice?

That's not how it works; inner rules do parse the actual content you specify inside them, they are not ignored like comments. The Pairs type is the standard Rust representation pest provides for this because it preserves the order and nesting of all parsed components including those in your inner rules. If you wanted to avoid duplication, you would use the raw_tokens method instead, but that gives you a flat list without the structural grouping that pairs provide.
Okay, I see now. But how do I handle WHITESPACE and COMMENT tokens? Do they get included in the Pairs map or should I filter them out manually?

WHITESPACE and COMMENT tokens are automatically included in the Pairs structure because pest treats them as regular symbols that can be paired, though you can use the whitespace::is_comment method to check if a token is a comment before adding it to your custom data structures. You should typically filter these out manually when building your visitor logic since they don't carry meaningful semantic information for most DSLs.
Good point on filtering them. Now I'm running into an issue with error reporting - when my parser fails, how do pest actually tell me which part of the grammar failed?

Error reporting in pest relies on the pair::error_span method to locate exactly where the parsing failure occurred by returning a Span object that includes start and end positions. You can combine this with the error::Error type returned by your parser function, then use the span's location data to generate helpful messages like 'Expected token X at line Y column Z' instead of generic errors.

I've been manually writing regex patterns for our log parser and it's becoming a nightmare to maintain. I tried using the Json wrapper from serde_json but realized it only handles JSON, not arbitrary text formats. We need something more robust like the pest crate which supports PEG parsing with its own grammar files instead of raw strings. I'm currently building a tool to automate repetitive log processing tasks and my current solution is brittle because every time we add a new field I have to manually update these regex patterns everywhere they appear in our codebase. Is there a better way to handle this kind of structural ambiguity?

The frustration you're feeling comes from trying to parse complex structures with tools designed for simple extraction. The pest crate solves this by letting you define your grammar rules in a .pest file where you can declare pairs and inner rules to group related tokens together, rather than wrestling with nested character classes in regex. When you use the parser combinator traits like Parser::new() or try_match(), you get proper error reporting that tells you exactly which token failed instead of giving you a generic 'no match' message. The key insight is that pest separates your logic from your syntax by using a dedicated grammar file format, so when you add new fields you only update the .pest definition and regenerate the parser rather than hunting through every source file. This approach also handles whitespace and comment stripping automatically if you declare them in the grammar, which prevents accidental mismatches in your logs.

That sounds much cleaner but I'm still confused about how pairs actually work versus just listing individual tokens. In my current implementation I'm treating every token as independent when really some of these are clearly grouped together like identifiers and operators that should be parsed as a single unit. Can you explain the difference between declaring something as a pair versus just a regular rule in the grammar file? And how does this impact the error reporting we saw earlier?

Pairs in pest allow you to group multiple tokens into a logical unit where they must appear consecutively, like an identifier followed by an operator. When you define a pair with inner rules, the parser treats that entire sequence as one atomic token for downstream processing which simplifies your code significantly compared to manually chaining individual token checks. This structural grouping is what makes error reporting meaningful because if any part of the pair fails the validation, pest reports exactly where the pair started and ended rather than just saying something was invalid. For example instead of getting a confusing error about an unexpected operator you'll see 'expected identifier or operator' at the precise location in your log line where that specific combination failed to match.
Hey, I'm trying to port our old parser into a .pest file for Rust. I thought if I just define the rules and put them in order, it should work like C++.

You're close but there's a gotcha: pest uses PEG (Recursive Descent) parsing which is stricter than LL or LR grammars. You can't just list rules in sequence; you need to define them explicitly and handle whitespace and comments so the parser doesn't choke on formatting.
Oh, I didn't realize whitespace matters that much. So if I don't declare WHITESPACE or COMMENT tokens in my grammar file, will pest just ignore them?

No, it won't ignore them and you'll get weird errors later. If your input contains spaces or comments but you haven't declared those rules, the parser treats them as syntax errors. You must explicitly declare WHITESPACE and COMMENT tokens in your .pest file so pest can skip over them gracefully.

I'm trying to build a lexer for my hobby project using the pest crate, but I keep getting confused about how pairs and inner rules interact. The docs say WHITESPACE gets stripped automatically, so why does my parser still complain when there are spaces between tokens?

It looks like you might be thinking of ANTLR's whitespace skipping behavior rather than how pest handles it. In .pest grammar files, WHITESPACE is just a rule name that tells pest to ignore matches for those tokens during parsing; it doesn't automatically strip text from your input stream before the parser runs. The actual stripping happens at the crate level if you configure the lexer appropriately.
Oh, I see! So pest itself just collects all tokens and lets me decide what to ignore? That makes sense compared to how my old C parser worked. But then why do I need pairs in the grammar file at all?

Great question about that distinction. Pairs are specifically designed for error recovery in pest because they allow you to define a rule like 'error' followed by 'rest_of_file' which helps the parser recover from unexpected tokens instead of crashing immediately. Without pairs, pest would just report an error and stop parsing.
That's really helpful! So if I want my lexer to handle comments that might appear anywhere in the code without breaking everything else, should I use pairs?

Yes, using pairs is exactly what you need for that scenario. You can define a pair rule like 'comment' followed by 'rest_of_file' which lets pest skip over any comment text and continue parsing the rest of your source code without interruption.
Okay, so I'll add those pairs to my grammar file. But wait, what about error reporting? Does pest automatically tell me which line caused the problem?

Pest does provide detailed error messages including line and column numbers by default when you run it with the --print-parse-tree flag or use the debug mode. However, if you want custom error messages that explain exactly what went wrong in your code, you'll need to implement a custom error handler using the pest_panic crate or configure the parser to call your own function.
